lessons.title: Sampling & the Nyquist Theorem
lessons.content:
Sampling is the bridge from the continuous analog world your radar front-end lives in to the discrete numbers a CPU or FPGA can process. Model **ideal sampling** as multiplying a continuous signal $x(t)$ by an impulse train (a Dirac comb) of period $T_s = 1/f_s$. Multiplication by a comb in time becomes **convolution with a comb** in frequency, so the spectrum $X(f)$ gets copied (replicated) at every multiple of the sample rate:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This spectral replication is the whole game. If the original signal is bandlimited to $B$ Hz, each replica is a triangle (schematically) sitting at $0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \dots$. As long as the replicas do **not overlap**, the baseband copy is a perfect, undistorted image of the original and you can recover $x(t)$ exactly.

The **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem** states the no-overlap condition: a signal bandlimited to $B$ can be perfectly reconstructed if

$$f_s > 2B.$$

The minimum rate $2B$ is the **Nyquist rate**; half the sample rate, $f_s/2$, is the **Nyquist frequency** (the folding frequency). Keep these straight - the Nyquist rate is a property of the *signal* (twice its bandwidth), while the Nyquist frequency is a property of your *sampler* (half its clock). A classic rust-knocker: you need to beat twice the *bandwidth*, not twice the highest frequency - that distinction is exactly what makes bandpass sampling possible later.

items.front: Model ideal sampling as a mathematical operation in the time domain.
items.back: Multiplication of $x(t)$ by an impulse train (Dirac comb) of period $T_s = 1/f_s$: $x_s(t) = x(t)\sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$.

items.front: What does multiplication by an impulse train in time do to the spectrum?
items.back: It convolves $X(f)$ with a frequency-domain comb, replicating (copying) the spectrum at every multiple of $f_s$: $X_s(f) = f_s\sum_k X(f - k f_s)$.

items.front: State the Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem condition for perfect reconstruction.
items.back: A signal bandlimited to $B$ Hz can be perfectly reconstructed if $f_s > 2B$ (replicas do not overlap).
Nyquist rate $= 2B$ is the minimum sample rate (a signal property). Nyquist frequency $= f_s/2$ is the folding frequency (a sampler property).

items.front: Why is sampling above twice the bandwidth, not twice the highest frequency, the precise statement?
items.back: The theorem constrains the spectral support (bandwidth $B$). For baseband signals the highest frequency equals $B$, but for bandpass signals the bandwidth can be far below the carrier - enabling undersampling.

items.prompt: A baseband signal is bandlimited to $B = 4$ kHz. What is the minimum (Nyquist) sample rate for perfect reconstruction?
If you sample at $f_s = 48$ kHz, what is the Nyquist (folding) frequency?

choices: Multiplying the spectrum by a constant
Convolving the spectrum with a comb (spectral replication)
Shifting the spectrum by a single fixed amount
items.explanation: Multiplication in time is convolution in frequency; convolving with a comb copies the spectrum at every multiple of $f_s$.
items.prompt: Multiplying a signal by an impulse train in the time domain corresponds in the frequency domain to:

items.explanation: No overlap requires $f_s > 2B$; this is exactly the Nyquist criterion for perfect reconstruction.
items.prompt: Replicas in the sampled spectrum will NOT overlap when:

lessons.title: Aliasing, Anti-Alias Filtering & Reconstruction
lessons.content:
When $f_s \le 2B$ the replicas overlap and high-frequency energy masquerades as low-frequency content. This is **aliasing**, and it is irreversible once it happens. The apparent (aliased) frequency of a tone at $f_{in}$ folds about the Nyquist frequency. A handy formula: the observed frequency is

$$f_{alias} = \left| f_{in} - f_s \cdot \operatorname{round}\!\left(\frac{f_{in}}{f_s}\right) \right|,$$

which produces the familiar triangular **fold** as $f_{in}$ sweeps up: it rises to $f_s/2$, then mirrors back down. Example: sampling a 7 kHz tone at $f_s = 10$ kHz gives $|7 - 10| = 3$ kHz - the 7 kHz tone *aliases* to 3 kHz and is indistinguishable from a real 3 kHz tone.

The defense is an **anti-alias filter (AAF)**: an analog low-pass filter placed *before* the ADC that attenuates everything above $f_s/2$ so it cannot fold into your band. Because real filters have finite roll-off, you usually oversample a bit to give the transition band room.

**Reconstruction** is the reverse trip. Perfect reconstruction multiplies the spectrum by an ideal brick-wall low-pass, which in time is convolution with a $\operatorname{sinc}$ - the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula**:

$$x(t) = \sum_n x[n]\,\operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right).$$

Real DACs cannot do an ideal sinc. A **zero-order hold (ZOH)** holds each sample for $T_s$, which imposes a $\operatorname{sinc}$-shaped amplitude droop $H(f) = \operatorname{sinc}(f/f_s)$ that a reconstruction (anti-imaging) filter then corrects.

items.front: What is aliasing, and is it reversible?
items.back: When $f_s \le 2B$, spectral replicas overlap and high frequencies fold into the band, masquerading as lower frequencies. It is irreversible once sampled.

items.front: Give the folding formula for the apparent (aliased) frequency of a tone $f_{in}$ sampled at $f_s$.
items.back: $f_{alias} = \left| f_{in} - f_s\,\operatorname{round}(f_{in}/f_s) \right|$ - energy folds (mirrors) about the Nyquist frequency $f_s/2$.

items.front: What is an anti-alias filter and where is it placed?
items.back: An analog low-pass filter placed BEFORE the ADC that attenuates energy above $f_s/2$ so it cannot fold into the band of interest.
$x(t) = \sum_n x[n]\,\operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ - ideal sinc interpolation, i.e. a brick-wall low-pass in frequency.

items.front: What spectral distortion does a zero-order-hold DAC introduce, and how is it fixed?
items.back: A $\operatorname{sinc}$ amplitude droop $H(f) = \operatorname{sinc}(f/f_s)$. A reconstruction (anti-imaging) filter, often with sinc-compensation, corrects it.

items.explanation: $|7 - 10| = 3$ kHz. The 7 kHz tone folds about $f_s/2 = 5$ kHz to 3 kHz.
items.prompt: A 7 kHz sinusoid is sampled at $f_s = 10$ kHz. What apparent frequency appears in the digitized data?

choices: To give the anti-alias filter a realistic finite transition band
To lower the DAC sinc droop to zero
items.explanation: Real anti-alias filters cannot roll off instantly; oversampling widens the transition region so out-of-band energy is adequately attenuated by $f_s/2$.
items.prompt: Why do designers oversample beyond the strict Nyquist minimum before an ADC?

items.explanation: An ideal brick-wall low-pass in frequency is a $\operatorname{sinc}$ in time, giving Whittaker-Shannon sinc interpolation.
items.prompt: Ideal reconstruction of a sampled signal corresponds in the time domain to interpolation with which function?

items.explanation: $|12 - 8 \cdot \operatorname{round}(12/8)| = |12 - 16| = 4$ kHz; equivalently it folds to 4 kHz.
items.prompt: A tone at 12 kHz is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz. What is its aliased frequency?

lessons.title: Bandpass Sampling, Quantization SNR & I/Q
lessons.content:
Modern SDR and radar receivers exploit a loophole in Nyquist: a **bandpass signal** of bandwidth $B$ centered at a high carrier need not be sampled at twice the carrier - only fast enough to keep its *replicas* from colliding. **Bandpass sampling (undersampling)** deliberately folds an RF band down to baseband. A sufficient condition is

$$\frac{2 f_H}{n} \le f_s \le \frac{2 f_L}{n-1},$$

where $f_L, f_H$ are the band edges and $n$ is an integer with $1 \le n \le \lfloor f_H/B \rfloor$. This is how a digital-IF receiver samples, say, a 70 MHz IF with a far lower clock.

**Quantization** maps each sample to one of $2^N$ levels for an $N$-bit ADC. The rounding error is modeled as uniform noise of variance $\Delta^2/12$ (with step $\Delta$). For a full-scale sinusoid this yields the canonical signal-to-quantization-noise ratio:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\ \text{dB}.$$

That is the famous **~6 dB per bit** rule: each added bit buys roughly 6 dB of dynamic range. **Dither** (adding small noise before quantizing) decorrelates the error and trades spurious tones for a smoother noise floor.

Finally, **I/Q (complex baseband)** represents a signal as in-phase and quadrature components $x(t) = I(t) + jQ(t)$. Sampling I and Q lets you capture a full bandwidth $B$ at a complex rate of just $B$ (vs. $2B$ real), preserving the sign of frequency - essential for Doppler, direction, and efficient decimation/interpolation in multirate chains.

items.front: What is bandpass sampling (undersampling) and why is it allowed?
items.back: Sampling a bandpass signal below twice its carrier, deliberately folding the RF band to baseband. Allowed because Nyquist only requires non-overlapping replicas (driven by bandwidth $B$), not $f_s > 2f_{carrier}$.

items.front: Give a valid sample-rate range for bandpass sampling with band edges $f_L, f_H$.
items.back: $\frac{2 f_H}{n} \le f_s \le \frac{2 f_L}{n-1}$ for integer $n$ with $1 \le n \le \lfloor f_H/B \rfloor$.

items.front: State the quantization SNR rule for a full-scale sinusoid into an N-bit ADC.
items.back: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 N + 1.76$ dB - roughly 6 dB of dynamic range per bit.

items.front: What is the variance of uniform quantization noise with step size $\Delta$?
items.back: $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$, assuming the error is uniformly distributed over one step.

items.front: What does dither do in a quantizer?
items.back: Adds small noise before quantizing to decorrelate the quantization error, converting harmonic spurs into a smoother (more white) noise floor.

items.front: What is I/Q (complex baseband) representation and a key advantage?
items.back: $x(t) = I(t) + jQ(t)$ with in-phase and quadrature components. It preserves the sign of frequency (positive vs. negative) and captures bandwidth $B$ at a complex sample rate of $B$ rather than $2B$ real.

items.prompt: Using the 6 dB/bit rule, what is the approximate full-scale-sinusoid SNR of a 12-bit ADC?
You need at least 60 dB of quantization SNR. Roughly how many ADC bits are required?

choices: It lets a lower-rate ADC capture a high-carrier signal by folding it to baseband
It removes the need for any anti-alias filter
items.explanation: Bandpass sampling folds an RF/IF band down to baseband, so the ADC clock need only satisfy the bandwidth-based replica condition, not twice the carrier.
items.prompt: What is the primary advantage of bandpass sampling in an SDR/radar receiver?

choices: Because complex samples carry I and Q per sample, resolving positive and negative frequencies
Because the carrier is removed by the ADC
Because dither doubles the effective rate
items.explanation: Each complex sample carries two real numbers (I and Q), distinguishing positive from negative frequencies, so a one-sided bandwidth $B$ needs a complex rate of $B$.
items.prompt: Why does I/Q (complex) sampling capture a bandwidth $B$ at a complex rate of only $B$ instead of $2B$?
